course_description:
This course uses a series of important "posts" (including post-modern, post-colonial, post-dramatic, post-human, post-feminist, and post-capitalist modules) as a framework to explore contemporary performance texts and forms. Students will study some of the most interesting plays and performance texts of the last two decades in order to begin thinking about the question: if we really are post-everything, then what exactly is the state of current national and international theatre?

Welcome to DRAM2040 Contemporary Theatre and Performance!

This semester you are going to be introduced to some of the most exciting contemporary performance texts from Australia and around the globe. You are also going to be introduced to important critical theory that provides substance to the series of "posts" we are using to frame your study of contemporary theatre. The onus is going to be upon you to maintain a rigorous reading load alongside your enjoyment of these plays and performances. You will be expected to arrive at class each week having read the articles AND the plays, and to be ready to immerse yourself in an informed exploration of all of this learning during tutorials. Lectures will provide the context for the theory and the performance texts and help you make sense of the former and connect it to the latter.

It sounds like a lot of work - and it will be. But it will also be an exhilarating ride through the pre- and post-millennial theatre scene. Our aim is to provide you with all the tools you need to become the most astute appreciator - or even maker! - of contemporary performance you can be.

from your
UQ student email
with:
Your name
Your student ID
The course code
A list of three tutorial preferences (in order of preference)
Reason for the change – e.g. timetable clash, elite athlete status, SAP
Teaching staff do not have access to change tutorials or help with timetables; all timetabling changes must be processed through the Timetabling Team.

learning_outcomes.description: Identify major concepts and practitioners in Australian and international contemporary performance.
Understand the differences between analysing dramatic scripts and contemporary performances (that are often multidisciplinary).

Understand the critical principles of postmodernism, postcolonialism, postdramatic theory, posthuman theory, and postfeminist and postcapitalist discourse; and be able to apply them to the creation and analysis of contemporary performance.
Understand the role of performance as an expression of and response to contemporary social and political contexts.
Employ appropriate research tools and critical reasoning to analyse texts, performances and performative processes in both theatre and non-theatre environments.
Work collaboratively to create a unique group performance that develops applied industry skills.

assessment_details.title: Participation
assessment_details.category: Participation/ Student contribution, Reflection, Tutorial/ Problem Set
assessment_details.due_date: 2/03/2026 - 11/05/2026 25/05/2026 - 25/05/2026 This is an in-person assessment completed during normal contact hours, including a 100-word in-class written activity.
assessment_details.task_description: This task assesses your engagement with the course across the Semester. Each week, your engagement with your course content and the tutorial activity (weeks 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 11) is worth 3%. For the first 2% of your mark between weeks 2 and 11, you will be assessed on the basis of three components: preparation for class; engagement with the learning activities in class; and respectful peer collaboration. You must bring a copy of the relevant text (e-copies are acceptable) and any additional material to every tutorial, and you must have read them at least once. Attendance itself is not sufficient to constitute 'participation'. Participation means willing involvement, active engagement with the text and the tasks at hand, asking questions, working well in groups, and demonstrating evidence of having listened to the lectures. To receive full marks, your tutor will need to see evidence of you having engaged with this full spectrum of activity. For the final 1% of your weekly mark between weeks 2 and 11 , you will write 100 words in class, responding to a specific prompt that draws on the lecture content and set texts (critical and performance). Some responses will require responses to visual and/or audio-visual material, and you will be provided with a worksheet to write on. Up to an additional 3% of marks is available for responding to a written prompt on a provided worksheet in the week 13 lecture. This may consist of short-answer questions of up to 100 words. Acting ability is not assessed. It is your responsibility to ensure that your attendance has been recorded. If you are absent from your tutorial through documented illness or other documented circumstances beyond your control, and supply this documentation to your tutor, you will receive a pass mark (in the range of a four) for that week. You may also be able to respond to the class activities and receive additional marks. Students will receive written feedback each week. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Essay
assessment_details.due_date: 7/04/2026 4:00 pm Submit via TurnItIn through Blackboard
assessment_details.task_description: This essay is designed to enable you to demonstrate your acquired knowledge and research skills by writing on a specific aspect of contemporary drama in relation to postcolonial theatre. You will write a 2000-word essay in response to the stimulus below, which will assess your ability to: produce a relevant and compelling argument provide an analytic approach to the topic and its relevant issues provide evidence from the text and secondary material to support this argument. Essay question: Discuss how postcolonialism and/or decolonisation intersect with postmodernism in contemporary theatre and performance, with reference to two pieces of contemporary drama. You need to choose one of the two set texts from our postcolonialism module, alongside one additional text. Your additional text can come from the supplied list, or you may choose your own text; if you choose your own additional text, you need to confirm its suitability with the course convenor by the end of Week Five. Set texts (choose one): Black Medea by Wesley Enoch OR seven methods for killing kylie jenner by Jasmine Lee-Jones AND Suggested texts (choose one from below, or something of your own choosing): An Octoroon by Branden Jacobs-Jenkins, OR Yellow Face by David Henry Hwang, OR Kill the Messenger by Nakkiah Lui, OR Burgerz by Travis Alabanza The referencing style used for Drama assignments is MLA, 9th edition. A detailed assignment brief will be provided via Blackboard at the beginning of Semester. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
TurnItIn Receipts:
Assignments for this course will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using TurnItIn . Before submitting any assignments for this course you must ensure you have completed UQ's compulsory online Academic Integrity Tutorial.
When you successfully submit your assessment to TurnItIn you will see text confirming your submission is complete, before being redirected to your Assignment inbox. On this page you can:
View the name of the submitted file View date and time of the upload Resubmit your paper (if necessary) Download your submitted paper Download digital receipt.
If you cannot see your submission in your Assignment inbox you should regard your submission as unsuccessful. Students are responsible for retaining evidence of submission by the due date for all assessment items, in the required form (e.g. screenshot, email, photo, and an unaltered copy of submitted work).
If the submission was not successful:
Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot). Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again. If you cannot submit again email your course coordinator immediately with the assignment attached.
Please visit this webpage for further advice on how to submit your TurnItIn assignment .
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Group Performance
assessment_details.due_date: 18/05/2026 Performances due in the tutorial. Performance rationale due in week 11 via Blackboard.
assessment_details.other_conditions: Peer assessment factor, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: The group performance project is designed to enable you to demonstrate your familiarity with one of the final two ‘posts’ that we studied in class this semester, and to apply that thinking to the understanding of postdramatic theatre that we have focused on throughout the course. In groups of four to six, you are required to devise a performance based on one set of the paired texts studied from weeks 7 to 11, using postdramatic techniques to explore your chosen texts, and their associated 'post' identifier. · Postfeminism · Posthumanism There is a 10-minute maximum performance time for this assignment. This short piece will be performed for other DRAM2040 students in Week 12. A 100-word performance rationale will be submitted by each group in week 11 outlining the aims of their performance. You will be provided with some technical support in the performance of your projects, with access to sound, lighting and video performance technology. You will also have access to rehearsal time in tutorials after the mid-semester break. Group members will each receive the same grade out of 35 for the performance. The remaining five marks will be determined through a peer-accountability survey, where you will be required to provide feedback on the work of your peers against criteria in BuddyCheck by the end of week 12. If you do not participate in the BuddyCheck survey, you will not receive the peer-accountability marks. A detailed assignment brief will be made available via Blackboard. Recording of Oral and Practical Assessment All presentations will be recorded for marking purposes via recording facilities available where the assessment takes place (eg. ECHO360, Zoom, camera device). Recordings will be retained by the School of Communication and Arts for at least 12 months from the release of the final grade for the course. Recordings will be stored in a secure manner and will only be accessed by authorised school staff for the purposes of: Moderation of marking; Provision of feedback to the student(s) recorded; and/or Re-marking following a successful re-mark application. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources: Find the required and recommended resources for this course on the UQ Library website.

learning_activities.topic: Week 1: Postmodernism, and Contemporary Theatre’s ‘Posts’ This introductory lecture will contextualise the 'posts' framework we use throughout DRAM2040, starting with an introduction to postmodernism.
Week 1: No Class
Week 2: The History and Context of Postdramatic Theatre This lecture will provide an introduction to postdramatic theatre.
Week 2: The History and Context of Postdramatic Theatre This tutorial will explore scenes and concepts related to postdramatic theatre, with reference to Martin Crimp's Attempts on her Life .
Week 3: Postdramatic Theatre in Australia An introduction to postdramatic theatre in an Australian setting.
Week 3: Postdramatic Theatre in Australia This tutorial will explore scenes and concepts related to postdramatic theatre, with reference to version 1.0's "CMI: A Certain Maritime Incident."
Week 4: Postcolonial Theory in an Australian setting Introduction to postcolonial theory as it applies to Australian theatre.
Week 4: Postcolonial Theory in an Australian setting This tutorial will explore scenes and concepts related to postcolonial theatre, with reference to Wesley Enoch's Black Medea.
Week 5: Black British Drama – Theatre in Postcolonial Britain Introduction to contemporary Black British drama.

Week 5: Black British Drama – Theatre in Postcolonial Britain This tutorial will explore scenes and concepts related to postcolonial theatre, with reference to Jasmine Lee-Jones' seven methods of killing kylie jenner .

Week 6: Immersivity and Interactivity, Making Sense of Theatre and Postcapitalism This week will draw on theories of postcapitalism to make sense of immersive and interactive trends in Contemporary theatre and performance.

Week 6: Immersivity and Interactivity, Making Sense of Theatre and Postcapitalism This week will draw on theories of postcapitalism to make sense of immersive and interactive trends in Contemporary theatre and performance using multimedia archives and textual records. Groups for the Group Performance assessment will be assigned.
Mid-Semester Break - No Class No lecture
Mid-Semester Break - No Class No tutorial
Week 7: Postfeminist Theatre – Grrrl Power Grrrrl Power. Introduction to the legacy of the in-yer-face movement and the dramaturgy of postfeminist playwriting.

Week 7: Postfeminist Theatre – Grrrl Power This tutorial will explore scenes and concepts related to postfeminist theatre, with reference to Alice Birch's Revolt. She Said. Revolt Again . Time in tutorials will be set aside for Group Performance rehearsals.
Week 8: Theatre and Critical Thinking, a Postfeminist Lens Development of post-feminist critical thinking as it applies to theatre, with reference to Ella Hickson's The Writer .
Week 8: Theatre and Critical Thinking, a Postfeminist Lens This tutorial will explore scenes and concepts related to postfeminist theatre, with reference to Ella Hickson's The Writer.
Week 9: Posthumanist Theatre – Cyborgs, Multimedia and Technology Cyborgs, multi-media, and technology - what does it mean to be post-human?

Week 9: Posthumanist Theatre – Cyborgs, Multimedia and Technology Using Sarah Ruhl's Dead Man's Cell Phone , concepts relating to extending the human will be explored. Time in tutorials will be set aside for Group Performance rehearsals.
Week 10: Reading Week No tutorial due to public holiday
Week 10: Reading Week No tutorial due to public holiday
Week 11: The Ethics of Posthumanism What happens when the posthuman comes to term with ethics?

Week 11: The Ethics of Posthumanism The ethical considerations of posthumanism will be examined through scene work from Jennifer Haley's The Nether. Time in tutorials will be set aside for Group Performance rehearsals.
Week 12: Rehearsal and tech time The lecture slot in Week 12 will be devoted to technical/dress rehearsals for the group performance assessment.
Week 12: Assessment of performances In-class performance assessment

Week 13. Post-postmodernism – AI and Theatre “Post” Everything This lecture will reflect on the course content and discussion as to the future directions of contemporary theatre and performance, with a focus on what AI means for theatre and performance. A writing task will be undertaken in the lecture as a contribution to up to 3% of the participation grades.
Week 13: No Class No tutorial

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . Course guidelines Communication Expectations While you are a student at UQ, all communication must be conducted according to the UQ Student Code of Conduct . The UQ Library has a helpful Communicate and collaborate online module . Email is the primary way for you to send messages to, and receive information from, the School and our staff. You must use your UQ email address (not a private address) to communicate with staff. You should add a clear subject line, including course code, and a 2-3 word statement. You can send email at any time, however please do not expect responses outside normal working hours (Monday to Friday from ~8am to ~5pm). Emails that constitute bullying, harassment or discrimination against staff contravene the Student Code of Conduct. Emails like this will be reported to the University, and the matter will be pursued as misconduct. Learning activities
